label: What type of application is being assessed?
instruction: Select the option that best describes the primary nature of this work.
help_text: Choose the category that most closely matches what is being built or changed.

label: What is the highest data sensitivity level processed by this application?
instruction: Select the most sensitive data classification that will be stored, processed, or transmitted.
help_text: If multiple sensitivity levels apply, choose the highest. PHI and financial data are Restricted.

label: What is the application's internet exposure?
instruction: Select the network boundary that best describes how the application is accessed.
help_text: Internet-facing includes any path reachable from the public internet, even via a load balancer or WAF.

label: What type of API integrations does the application use?
instruction: Select the API integration pattern that applies to this application.
help_text: API Provider means this service exposes endpoints that other systems call. API Consumer means this service calls external endpoints.

label: What types of users interact with the application?
instruction: Select the user population that will authenticate to and use this application.
help_text: Service Accounts Only means the application has no human users — only machine-to-machine interactions.

label: What level of third-party dependencies does the application have?
instruction: Select the primary type of external dependency this application relies on.
help_text: Managed Services includes cloud-native services like AWS S3, Azure AD, or GCP Pub/Sub that are operated by the provider.

label: Is multi-factor authentication enforced for all user accounts?
instruction: Answer Yes only if MFA is mandatory and cannot be disabled by the user.
help_text: Service-account-only applications may answer Not Applicable if no human login exists.

label: How mature is your password policy and enforcement practice?
instruction: Rate the completeness and automation of your password complexity, length, and breach-detection controls.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated enforcement of length, complexity, breached-password list checks, and periodic forced rotation for privileged accounts.

label: How mature is your credential storage practice?
instruction: Rate your use of adaptive hashing, unique salting, and key derivation for stored credentials.
help_text: Tier 3 means using bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2id with per-user salts and work-factor tuning reviewed annually.

label: Are default or shared credentials prohibited in all environments including development and staging?
instruction: Answer Yes if credentials are unique per user and environment, and defaults are changed or removed at provisioning.
help_text: CI/CD secrets and service accounts must also use unique non-default credentials rotated on a schedule.

label: Are session tokens generated with a cryptographically secure random generator?
instruction: Answer Yes if session IDs use a CSPRNG with at least 128 bits of entropy.
help_text: Sequential or predictable session IDs allow session prediction attacks. Use os.urandom or equivalent.

label: Is session timeout enforced after a defined period of inactivity?
instruction: Answer Yes if sessions expire automatically after idle time and the timeout is documented.
help_text: Recommended maximum idle timeout is 15-30 minutes for sensitive applications.

label: How mature is your session revocation and invalidation practice?
instruction: Rate your ability to immediately revoke all active sessions for a given user on demand.
help_text: Tier 3 means server-side session store with instant revocation, token blocklist for JWTs, and automated revocation on password change or account lockout.

label: Is authentication state validated on every protected request?
instruction: Answer Yes if every request to a protected resource re-validates the session or token server-side.
help_text: Client-side-only auth checks are insufficient. Middleware or decorator-based enforcement on every route is required.

label: Is role-based access control implemented with least-privilege defaults?
instruction: Answer Yes if new accounts start with minimal permissions and access must be explicitly granted.
help_text: Deny-by-default means no permission is inherited without explicit assignment. Wildcard grants are prohibited.

label: How mature is your access control review and recertification practice?
instruction: Rate how frequently and systematically access grants are reviewed and revoked when no longer needed.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated quarterly recertification with manager approval workflows and automatic revocation on no-response.

label: Are administrative functions restricted to verified administrator accounts?
instruction: Answer Yes if admin endpoints require a separate privileged role distinct from regular user roles.
help_text: Admin capability must not be accessible via standard user tokens. Separate auth flows for privileged operations are preferred.

label: Is object-level authorization enforced on every data access operation?
instruction: Answer Yes if every read, write, or delete verifies the requester owns or has permission for the specific object.
guideline: OWASP API Security Top 10 BOLA (API1), OWASP ASVS 4.2
help_text: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) is the most common API vulnerability. ID-based lookups must always check ownership.

label: Are authorization checks enforced server-side on every request?
instruction: Answer Yes if access decisions are made exclusively in server-side code and cannot be bypassed by client manipulation.
help_text: Client-side role checks in JavaScript or mobile code are cosmetic only and must not be the sole enforcement point.

label: How mature is your privilege escalation detection practice?
instruction: Rate your ability to detect and alert when a user attempts to access resources above their privilege level.
help_text: Tier 3 means real-time alerting with automated account suspension on repeated escalation attempts and post-incident forensic review.

label: Is the principle of deny-by-default applied to all access decisions?
instruction: Answer Yes if undefined or unmatched access requests result in denial rather than implicit permission.
help_text: Allowlist-based access control (explicitly permit what is allowed) is more secure than denylist-based (block what is known bad).

label: Are cross-tenant data access boundaries enforced at the data layer?
instruction: Answer Yes if tenant isolation is implemented in queries and data access code, not only in UI layer.
guideline: OWASP ASVS 4.2, OWASP API Security Top 10 API3
help_text: For single-tenant applications, answer Not Applicable. Multi-tenant systems must filter by tenant_id in every query.

label: How mature is your data classification practice for all data assets processed by this application?
instruction: Rate how completely and consistently data is classified and how classification drives downstream protection controls.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated classification tooling, classification labels enforced by policy engine, and quarterly review of classification decisions.

label: How mature is your data inventory and data flow mapping practice?
instruction: Rate how completely you have catalogued what data is stored, where it flows, and which systems touch it.
help_text: Tier 3 means an automated, continuously updated data map covering all data stores, pipelines, and third-party destinations.

label: Are data retention and disposal policies defined and enforced for all data classifications?
instruction: Answer Yes if retention schedules exist per data class and disposal is verified (e.g., cryptographic erasure or certified destruction).
help_text: Retention policy must cover backups and replicas, not only primary storage.

label: Is encryption enforced for all sensitive data at rest?
instruction: Answer Yes if Confidential and Restricted data is encrypted in all storage tiers including backups.
help_text: Encryption at rest must cover database volumes, object storage, and file systems. Transparent Disk Encryption alone may not be sufficient for column-level secrets.

label: Is TLS 1.2 or higher enforced on all data in transit?
instruction: Answer Yes if TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are explicitly disabled and all internal and external traffic uses TLS 1.2+.
help_text: Internal microservice traffic must also use TLS. Self-signed certificates are acceptable for internal traffic if certificate pinning or CA verification is implemented.

label: How mature is your cryptographic key management practice?
instruction: Rate your key lifecycle management including generation, storage, rotation, and revocation.
help_text: Tier 3 means keys stored in HSM or managed KMS with automated rotation, split knowledge for master keys, and audited key access.

label: Are deprecated or weak cryptographic algorithms explicitly prohibited in all system components?
instruction: Answer Yes if MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, and RSA key lengths below 2048 bits are blocked by policy and configuration.
help_text: Algorithm agility means the system can be updated to stronger algorithms without architectural changes. Hardcoded algorithm choices are a red flag.

label: Is personally identifiable information masked or anonymized in non-production environments?
instruction: Answer Yes if development, staging, and test environments never contain real PII.
help_text: Synthetic data generation or data masking pipelines should be used for non-production seeding. Copying production databases to dev is prohibited.

label: How mature is your data loss prevention practice?
instruction: Rate your controls that detect and prevent unauthorized exfiltration of sensitive data.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated DLP tooling inspecting egress channels with alerting, blocking, and quarterly tuning of detection rules.

label: Are backups encrypted and access-controlled with the same rigor as primary data?
instruction: Answer Yes if backup data is encrypted at rest and access is restricted to authorized backup administrators.
help_text: Unencrypted backups are a common data breach vector. Backup encryption keys must be stored separately from the backup data itself.

label: Is all user-supplied input validated against an allowlist or schema before processing?
instruction: Answer Yes if input validation is enforced server-side using type, length, format, and range checks before any processing occurs.
help_text: Allowlist validation (accept only known-good formats) is stronger than denylist (block known-bad patterns). Validation must happen before any business logic executes.

label: Is output encoding applied to prevent cross-site scripting in all rendering contexts?
instruction: Answer Yes if dynamic data is context-specifically encoded (HTML, JS, CSS, URL) before being included in responses.
guideline: OWASP ASVS 5.3, OWASP XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
help_text: Context-aware encoding means HTML encoding for HTML content, JS encoding for script contexts, and URL encoding for URL parameters. A single encoding function does not cover all contexts.

label: How mature is your input validation testing practice?
instruction: Rate the depth and automation of testing that specifically targets input validation boundaries.
guideline: OWASP ASVS 5.1, OWASP Testing Guide WSTG-INPV
help_text: Tier 3 means automated fuzzing in CI/CD, boundary value tests for all input fields, and regular penetration testing targeting injection vectors.

label: Are file uploads validated for type, size, and content before storage or processing?
instruction: Answer Yes if uploaded files are validated by magic bytes (not filename extension), capped by size, and scanned before use.
guideline: OWASP ASVS 12.1, OWASP File Upload Cheat Sheet
help_text: Extension-based validation is easily bypassed. Magic byte inspection and AV scanning of uploaded content are required for Confidential and Restricted data environments.

label: Are parameterized queries or ORM methods used for all database access operations?
instruction: Answer Yes if no SQL, NoSQL, or LDAP query is constructed by concatenating user-supplied input with a query string.
help_text: Parameterized queries separate data from code at the database driver level. String interpolation in queries is never acceptable regardless of preceding validation.

label: Is server-side request forgery prevention implemented for all external URL handling?
instruction: Answer Yes if user-supplied URLs are validated against an allowlist of permitted destinations before server-side fetch.
guideline: OWASP SSRF Prevention Cheat Sheet, OWASP API Security Top 10 API7
help_text: SSRF allows attackers to probe internal infrastructure. Validate scheme, host, and port against an allowlist. DNS rebinding attacks require re-validation after DNS resolution.

label: Are command injection vectors mitigated for all system command execution paths?
instruction: Answer Yes if no shell command is constructed from user input, or all such cases use argument arrays without shell interpretation.
help_text: Avoid subprocess calls with shell=True in Python, exec() in Node.js, or Runtime.exec() with string arguments in Java. Use argument arrays that bypass shell interpretation.

label: How mature is your deserialization safety practice?
instruction: Rate your controls preventing unsafe deserialization of attacker-controlled data.
guideline: OWASP ASVS 5.5, CWE-502, OWASP Deserialization Cheat Sheet
help_text: Tier 3 means schema-validated deserialization with type allowlists, no deserialization of untrusted data to executable types, and automated dependency scanning for deserialization gadget chains.

label: Are security-relevant events logged including authentication attempts, authorization decisions, and sensitive data access?
instruction: Answer Yes if login success/failure, access denials, and access to Confidential or Restricted data are all captured in logs.
help_text: Logs must include timestamp, user identity, source IP, resource accessed, and outcome. Logging only failures misses insider threat patterns.

label: How mature is your centralized log aggregation practice?
instruction: Rate how consistently logs from all application components are aggregated into a central, queryable store.
help_text: Tier 3 means all components stream to a SIEM or log management platform in real-time with structured (JSON) log format and searchable within 60 seconds of emission.

label: Is log data protected against tampering and unauthorized access?
instruction: Answer Yes if logs are write-once or write-append-only, access is restricted, and integrity mechanisms (hashing or WORM) are in place.
help_text: Log tampering is a key attacker technique for covering tracks. Logs must not be stored where the application or compromised service account can delete them.

label: Are log retention periods defined, documented, and enforced for all log types?
instruction: Answer Yes if a documented retention schedule exists and automated deletion or archiving enforces it.
help_text: Minimum retention for security logs is typically 90 days hot (immediately queryable) and 1 year cold (archived). Regulated industries may require longer periods.

label: How mature is your security alert and incident detection practice?
instruction: Rate how effectively security-relevant log events trigger alerts and initiate incident response workflows.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated correlation rules, anomaly detection, alert routing to on-call with documented SLAs, and post-incident review closing the detection loop.

label: Are audit logs available for compliance review and forensic investigation within defined SLAs?
instruction: Answer Yes if logs are retrievable within a documented timeframe for compliance auditors and incident responders.
help_text: Forensic readiness means logs can answer: who did what, when, from where, and with what result. Test retrieval procedures periodically.

label: Is rate limiting implemented on all public-facing endpoints?
instruction: Answer Yes if all unauthenticated and authenticated public endpoints enforce per-client request rate limits.
guideline: STRIDE DoS, OWASP API Security Top 10 API4, NIST SP 800-53 SC-5
help_text: Rate limiting must be enforced at the infrastructure layer (WAF, API gateway) not only in application code, so it survives application-level DoS conditions.

label: How mature is your disaster recovery practice?
instruction: Rate how formally your recovery time objective (RTO) and recovery point objective (RPO) are defined and tested.
help_text: Tier 3 means documented RTO/RPO approved by stakeholders, automated failover tested quarterly, and DR runbooks reviewed after each test.

label: Is graceful degradation implemented for dependent service failures?
instruction: Answer Yes if the application continues to provide partial service or a clear error state when upstream dependencies are unavailable.
help_text: Circuit breaker patterns, fallback responses, and health-check-based routing are common implementation approaches. Cascading failures to end users indicate absent graceful degradation.

label: Are resource limits configured to prevent CPU, memory, and connection exhaustion?
instruction: Answer Yes if container or process resource limits, connection pool maximums, and timeout values are explicitly configured.
help_text: Unbounded resource consumption allows a single misbehaving client or bug to bring down the entire service. Limits must be set below the point of system failure.

label: How mature is your capacity planning and performance monitoring practice?
instruction: Rate how proactively you forecast resource needs and detect degradation before it impacts availability.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated capacity forecasting from historical metrics, proactive scaling policies, and SLO-based alerting that fires before SLA breach.

label: Is automated health checking and self-healing implemented for all critical service components?
instruction: Answer Yes if health endpoints are actively polled and failing instances are automatically replaced or restarted without manual intervention.
help_text: Liveness and readiness probes (Kubernetes), load balancer health checks, and watchdog processes are common implementations. Manual restarts during incidents indicate absent self-healing.

label: Is authentication required on all API endpoints that process or return sensitive data?
instruction: Answer Yes only if every endpoint that touches sensitive data enforces token or key validation server-side. Public status/health endpoints are exempt.
help_text: Answer Yes only if every endpoint that touches sensitive data enforces token or key validation server-side. Public status/health endpoints are exempt.

label: Are API keys and tokens validated with each request and rejected when expired or revoked?
instruction: Answer Yes if token revocation is enforced server-side and JWTs are verified for both signature and expiry on every call.
help_text: Token revocation must be server-side, not client-side. JWTs must be verified for signature and expiry on every call.

label: How mature is your API secret and key rotation practice?
instruction: Rate how systematically API keys and secrets are rotated, with zero-downtime handoff and audit logging of all rotation events.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated rotation on a defined schedule, with zero-downtime key handoff and audit logging of all rotation events.

label: Is mutual TLS (mTLS) or equivalent certificate-pinning enforced for high-sensitivity service-to-service API calls?
instruction: Answer Yes if mTLS or certificate pinning is in place for APIs that process PII, financial, or regulated data. Internal-only low-sensitivity APIs may answer Not Applicable.
help_text: Required for APIs that process PII, financial, or regulated data. Internal-only low-sensitivity APIs may answer Not Applicable.

label: Is input validated and schema-enforced at all API entry points before processing?
instruction: Answer Yes if validation occurs server-side, including OpenAPI or JSON schema enforcement at the gateway layer.
guideline: OWASP API Security Top 10 API3, ASVS 5.1
help_text: Validation must occur server-side. OpenAPI/JSON schema enforcement at the gateway layer counts.

label: Is rate limiting enforced on all API endpoints to prevent abuse and denial-of-service?
instruction: Answer Yes if rate limits apply per-client (by IP or API key), are documented, and have been tested.
help_text: Rate limits must apply per-client (by IP or API key), not globally. Limits must be documented and tested.

label: Are API versions managed explicitly, with deprecated versions decommissioned on a documented schedule?
instruction: Answer Yes if no shadow or zombie APIs exist in production — all endpoints are versioned and deprecated versions follow a documented retirement schedule.
help_text: Shadow or zombie APIs (undocumented, unversioned endpoints) must not exist in production.

label: How mature is your API security testing practice (DAST, API fuzzing, contract testing)?
instruction: Rate the automation and depth of API-specific security testing in your development lifecycle.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated API security scanning in CI, contract tests per endpoint, and quarterly manual penetration test of the API surface.

label: Are all user-supplied values HTML-encoded before rendering in the browser?
instruction: Answer Yes if server-side encoding is mandatory and framework auto-escaping (React JSX, Jinja2 autoescape) is enabled by default.
help_text: Server-side encoding is mandatory. Framework auto-escaping (React JSX, Jinja2 autoescape) counts when enabled by default.

label: Is CSRF protection implemented on all state-changing operations?
instruction: Answer Yes if CSRF tokens or SameSite=Strict/Lax cookies protect every form submission and AJAX mutation.
help_text: CSRF tokens or SameSite=Strict/Lax cookies must protect every form submission or AJAX mutation.

label: Are session cookies marked HttpOnly and Secure, and transmitted only over TLS?
instruction: Answer Yes if all session cookies carry the HttpOnly and Secure attributes and are never transmitted over plain HTTP.
help_text: Cookies accessible to JavaScript (missing HttpOnly) are vulnerable to XSS-driven session theft.

label: How mature is your client-side dependency and third-party script management practice?
instruction: Rate how systematically you track, audit, and protect against vulnerable or malicious third-party scripts.
help_text: Tier 3 means Subresource Integrity (SRI) on all CDN scripts, automated npm audit in CI, and a content security policy blocking inline scripts.

label: Is a Content Security Policy (CSP) header enforced in production with no unsafe-inline directives?
instruction: Answer Yes if CSP is in enforcement mode (not report-only) and unsafe-inline does not appear in the script-src directive.
help_text: Report-only mode does not count. Nonce-based or hash-based CSP is preferred. unsafe-inline must not appear in script-src.

label: Are HTTP security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy) configured on all responses?
instruction: Answer Yes if all four headers are present on every response, with HSTS max-age of at least one year and includeSubDomains.
help_text: HSTS must have a max-age of at least one year with includeSubDomains. X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors blocks clickjacking.

label: Is TLS enforced end-to-end with no fallback to HTTP for all user-facing endpoints?
instruction: Answer Yes if HTTP to HTTPS redirect produces a 301 (permanent redirect) and no mixed-content warnings exist.
help_text: HTTP to HTTPS redirect must produce a 301 (permanent). Mixed-content warnings must be absent.

label: How mature is your automated web security testing practice (DAST, accessibility scanning, penetration testing)?
instruction: Rate the depth and automation of web-specific security testing in your development lifecycle.
help_text: Tier 3 means DAST in CI on every merge, quarterly manual penetration test, and accessibility scan (WCAG AA) on every release.

label: Is a software composition analysis (SCA) tool run automatically on every build to detect vulnerable dependencies?
instruction: Answer Yes if an SCA tool (Dependabot, Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check, Black Duck) runs in CI on every build. A manual quarterly review does not satisfy this control.
help_text: SCA tools include Dependabot, Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check, Black Duck. A manual quarterly review does not satisfy this control.

label: Is a software bill of materials (SBOM) generated and retained for each production release?
instruction: Answer Yes if a machine-readable SBOM (SPDX or CycloneDX format) is generated at build time and retained for the life of each software version.
help_text: SBOM must be machine-readable (SPDX or CycloneDX). It must be retained for the life of the software version.

label: How mature is your dependency patching and upgrade practice?
instruction: Rate how systematically vulnerable dependencies are identified and remediated with defined SLAs.
help_text: Tier 3 means automated PR creation for dependency patches, SLA of 7 days for critical CVEs, and signed release artifacts.

label: Are third-party vendors and service providers assessed for security posture before integration?
instruction: Answer Yes if each vendor provides evidence of security controls (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 cert, or equivalent questionnaire) before integration is approved.
help_text: Assessment must include evidence of security controls (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 cert, or equivalent questionnaire). No assessment = No.

label: Are vendor security assessments repeated on a defined schedule (at minimum annually) and on significant contract renewals?
instruction: Answer Yes if ongoing monitoring or periodic reassessment occurs. A one-time onboarding assessment does not count.
help_text: One-time onboarding assessment does not count. Ongoing monitoring or periodic reassessment is required.

label: How mature is your vendor contractual security requirements practice?
instruction: Rate how comprehensively security obligations, right-to-audit clauses, and breach notification SLAs are embedded in vendor contracts.
help_text: Tier 3 means security requirements are embedded in every vendor contract, with right-to-audit clauses and breach notification SLAs.

label: Is a designated security owner or CISO accountable for this application's security posture?
instruction: Answer Yes if a named individual with documented accountability is responsible for this application's security. An on-call rotation without a named owner does not count.
help_text: Accountability must be documented. An on-call rotation without a named owner does not count.

label: Is a documented incident response plan in place that covers this application, with defined escalation paths?
instruction: Answer Yes if the plan identifies roles, contact lists, and steps for detection, containment, eradication, and recovery. Untested plans should be answered No.
help_text: The plan must identify roles, contact lists, and steps for detection, containment, eradication, and recovery. Untested plans should be answered No.

label: How mature is your security incident response exercise and drills practice?
instruction: Rate how regularly incident response plans are exercised with tabletop drills and post-incident reviews.
help_text: Tier 3 means tabletop exercises at least annually, with a post-incident review, documented lessons learned, and plan updates.

label: Have applicable regulatory compliance obligations (GDPR, PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOX, etc.) been formally identified and mapped to controls?
instruction: Answer Yes if a compliance matrix exists that maps each regulation to specific controls. Informal knowledge does not count.
help_text: A compliance matrix must exist. Informal knowledge does not count.

label: Is security awareness training mandatory for all personnel with access to this application or its data?
instruction: Answer Yes if training is required before access is granted and refreshed at least annually, with evidence of completion retained.
help_text: Training must be completed before access is granted and refreshed at least annually. Evidence of completion must be retained.

label: How mature is your security policy review and update practice?
instruction: Rate how systematically security policies are reviewed, updated, and approved on a defined cycle.
help_text: Tier 3 means policy review on a defined annual cycle, triggered also by significant incidents or regulatory changes, with an audit trail of approvals.
